title: Deconstructing
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro

outline.title: Introduction: Deconstruction and Its Biblical Name
subpoints: Post-COVID phenomenon: nearly epidemic among contemporary Christian artists abandoning faith; various reasons given — church abuse, hell, guilt over sin
Demas's example (v.10) illustrates the core reason: 'having loved this present world' — literally 'having loved the now age' (Greek: ton nyn aiona)
Deconstruction is not new: the Bible calls it apostasy — a falling away from the faith, examined through the lens of Michel Foucault and Jacques Derrida but rooted in an old problem

outline.title: 1. Paul's Situation: Dungeon, Death, and Ministry (2 Tim 4:9-18)
subpoints: Second Roman imprisonment: not house arrest (as in Acts 28) but an actual dungeon; Paul has already appeared before the Imperial Tribunal (v.16); expects execution soon
Verse 6: ready to be 'poured out as a drink offering' — a poetic way of saying he expects to be beheaded; yet he still thinks about ministry: bring Mark, bring books and parchments
Loneliness: Crescens, Titus, and Tychicus have gone elsewhere; only Luke remains; but what weighs on Paul is Demas

outline.title: 2. Demas: The Anatomy of Apostasy
subpoints: Demas was once Paul's fellow worker (Philemon 24; Colossians 4:14) — a gospel worker alongside the apostle
Paul says he 'forsook' him: the Greek egkataleipō means utter abandonment — the same word Jesus used from the cross: 'My God, my God, why have you forsaken me?' (Matthew 27:46)

The stated cause: 'having loved the now age' (ton nyn aiona) — not the created world but the values and ideas consistent with this age, the age characterized by the rule of Satan (Ephesians 2:2; Ephesians 6:12; 2 Corinthians 4:3-4)
People who deconstruct give many reasons — abuse, hell, guilt — but the common thread is: they see something in the now age more attractive than what Christ offers in the age to come

outline.title: 3. Three Areas of Worldly Attraction (1 John 2:15-17)
subpoints: John uses 'world' (kosmos) in the same sense Paul uses 'now age' — the age under Satan's governance, contrasted with eternity

Three areas of temptation: the lusts of the flesh (desires leftover from the old sinful nature); the lusts of the eyes (temptations from outside); the pride of life (material life, possessions, love of money)
Worldliness is human nature without God — to love the now age is to act as if you had no new nature in Christ; sanctification is the process of putting off the old and putting on the new

outline.title: 4. The Church Has Forgotten Heaven
subpoints: Deconstruction has become popular partly because the church has forgotten the age to come: up to 1950, roughly 98% of hymns sung in church were about heaven and the life to come; today only about 2% are
We preach and pursue the practical now — how to live now, how to succeed now — while neglecting the life to come; but we can only be of any good now if we are heavenly-minded

Jonathan Lehman: Christians are 'time travelers' — citizens of the age to come who have traveled back to the now age, living thoughts and beliefs from then; Peter calls us to live as soldiers in a foreign land, because we are going somewhere else
The winsome testimony to those deconstructing: be excited about heaven, the resurrection, and seeing Jesus; when the church is consumed with the life to come, the now age loses its power to deceive

summary: A Sunday evening sermon prompted by the post-COVID wave of 'deconstruction' among professing Christians, particularly among well-known Christian artists. The text is 2 Timothy 4:9-18: Paul writing from a Roman dungeon, expecting execution, and noting that Demas has 'forsaken' him. The Greek word for 'forsake' (egkataleipō) is the same word Jesus used from the cross — 'My God, why have you forsaken me?' — indicating utter abandonment, not mere departure. Paul's stated cause: Demas 'loved the now age' (Greek: ton nyn aiona) — not the created world but the values of the age under Satan's rule (Ephesians 2:2; 6:12; 2 Corinthians 4:3-4). This is the biblical category for deconstruction: apostasy. However people explain their departure (abuse, questions about hell, guilt), the common thread is finding something in the now age more attractive than the age to come. First John 2:15-17 names the three areas: lusts of the flesh, lusts of the eyes, and pride of life. The root diagnosis: the church has forgotten heaven. Up to 1950, approximately 98% of hymns sung in church were about heaven and the life to come; today only about 2% are. Jonathan Lehman describes Christians as 'time travelers' — citizens of the age to come living in the present. The prescription: grow excited about heaven, the resurrection, and Christ himself, so that the now age loses its power to deceive.
transcript:
## Introduction: Deconstruction

Turn to Second Timothy chapter 4. Starting at verse 9, reading through verse 18. This is the word of our Lord.

*Be diligent to come to me quickly, for Demas has forsaken me, having loved this present world, and has departed for Thessalonica — Crescens for Galatia, Titus for Dalmatia. Only Luke is with me. Get Mark and bring him with you, for he is useful to me for ministry. And Tychicus I have sent to Ephesus. Bring the cloak that I left with Carpus at Troas when you come — and the books, especially the parchments. Alexander the coppersmith did me much harm. May the Lord repay him according to his works. You also must beware of him, for he has greatly resisted our words. At my first defense no one stood with me, but all forsook me. May it not be charged against them. But the Lord stood with me and strengthened me, so that the message might be preached fully through me, and all the Gentiles might hear. Also I was delivered out of the mouth of the lion. And the Lord will deliver me from every evil work and preserve me for his heavenly kingdom. To him be glory forever and ever. Amen.*

There is a lot of talk these days about Christians deconstructing their faith. Post-COVID, this has reached almost epidemic proportions among contemporary Christian artists — many abandoning the faith, saying they are deconstructing.

All kinds of reasons are given: abuse in the church, the concept of hell, guilt over sin. But Demas's example illustrates the core reason people leave Christianity. At the core of deconstruction is love for this present age. And this deconstruction is nothing new. The Bible calls it apostasy — a falling away from the faith.

## 1. Paul's Situation

Paul is in Rome for the second time — not under house arrest as in Acts 28, but in an actual dungeon. He has appeared before the Imperial Tribunal (v.16). Things are looking bleak.

In verse 6 of this chapter he says he is ready to be poured out as a drink offering — his way of saying he expects to be beheaded soon. His life and ministry are coming to an end. And yet he is still thinking about how to minister. He wants Mark brought to him, the same Mark he refused to take on a missionary journey and fought with Barnabas over — because Mark is now useful for the ministry. He is cold, so he asks for his cloak. He loves reading and writing, so he asks for books and parchments. He wants to keep working.

But what weighs heavily on his heart is Demas.

## 2. The Anatomy of Apostasy

Verse 10: *For Demas has forsaken me, having loved this present world.*

Demas was Paul's fellow worker. Philemon 24 calls him that. He helped Paul proclaim the gospel of Jesus Christ. And now he has left.

The word Paul uses — *forsaken* — is the Greek *egkataleipō*. It means utter abandonment. And it is the same word Jesus used from the cross: *My God, my God, why have you forsaken me?* That same word. Demas utterly abandoned Paul.

And the reason: he *loved the now age.* Paul does not say 'the world' in the way we might mean the earth or the environment. He uses the Greek phrase *ton nyn aiona* — the now age. The age characterized by the rule of Satan. Throughout Paul's letters, Satan is called the prince of the power of the air (Ephesians 2:2), the ruler of the darkness of this age (Ephesians 6:12), the god of this age who blinds the minds of unbelievers (2 Corinthians 4:3-4). Demas loved that age more than the age to come.

When you talk to people who are deconstructing, they give all kinds of reasons. Abuse in church. Hell. Guilt. But when you look at all those reasons, the common thread is: they see something in the now age that is more attractive to them than what is to come with Jesus Christ. Whatever the stated reason, what is actually going on is: I love this more than I want what's to come.

## 3. Three Areas: 1 John 2:15-17

Turn to First John chapter 2, verses 15 through 17.

*Do not love the world or the things in the world. If anyone loves the world, the love of the Father is not in him. For all that is in the world — the lust of the flesh, the lust of the eyes, and the pride of life — is not of the Father but is of the world. And the world is passing away and the lust of it, but he who does the will of God abides forever.*

John uses *cosmos* here — the same concept Paul calls 'the now age,' contrasted with eternity. Don't love the now age. Don't love this age characterized by the rule of Satan. Another way to say it: don't be worldly. Don't think, act, or believe as if you exist without God.

In Christ we are new creatures with a new nature and a new identity. To love the now age is to act according to an identity that is no longer ours. Paul says: if anyone is in Christ, he is a new creation — old things have passed away, behold all things have become new. But there are vestiges of the old nature we are still working through in sanctification.

John identifies three areas where we struggle: the lusts of the flesh (desires left over from our fallen nature), the lusts of the eyes (temptations that assault us from outside), and the pride of life (material life, possessions, money, status).

## 4. The Church Has Forgotten Heaven

I am convinced, as I read the testimonies of people who have deconstructed and as I listen to what philosophers are saying, that deconstruction has become popular because the church has forgotten heaven.

We are about the now. We have sermons that teach us how to live now, how to succeed now — that is what Christians want. Give me the practical stuff. How do I live now. And the focus is never on the life to come. But we can only be of any good now if we are heavenly-minded.

This is even seen in the songs we sing. Up to 1950, roughly 98% of the songs sung in church were about heaven and the life to come. That has completely flipped: today only about 2% of songs sung in church are about heaven and the life to come. We are too focused on here. Too focused on now.

So if the church is all about now, why would the world bother being part of the church? As well to go eat, drink, and be merry, for tomorrow we die — if there's no difference between the church and the world.

Jonathan Lehman says that we are to live as if we had traveled in time. We are citizens of the age to come who have traveled back to the now age and are living thoughts and beliefs that come from then. Peter gives the same image: live as a soldier in a foreign land, because this is not our world — we are going somewhere else.

Deconstruction often happens because people are so attached to the now. If we want to be a testimony to those who are considering abandoning the faith, we need to be excited about the life to come. Excited about heaven. Excited about seeing Jesus face to face. Excited about the resurrection. Less concerned about worldly occupations and duties, more consumed with Jesus — and when we do that, then everything in this life becomes about moving toward him.

That is the testimony: you don't have to deconstruct your faith, because the now age is not worth it. There is an age to come and we are going there.
